case-for-readable-labels.label: CASE for Readable Labels
case-for-readable-labels.summary: Turn row-level values into clear categories while handling missing data before numeric thresholds.

sketch1.title: Order Thresholds from Most Specific to Broadest

single-choice-1.title: First Matching Branch
single-choice-1.prompt: How does a searched CASE choose its result?
single-choice-1.hint: CASE evaluates conditions in written order.

help.concept: The first true WHEN determines the result.
help.hint_1: Later branches are skipped after a match.
help.hint_2: Choose the behavior that makes order important.

options.b: It returns the first matching branch.

single-choice-2.title: Missing Rating Label
single-choice-2.prompt: Why should `customer_rating IS NULL` appear before rating thresholds?
single-choice-2.hint: A missing observation does not communicate the same meaning as a poor score.

help.concept: Explicit NULL handling protects the business interpretation.
help.hint_1: The report should not invent a low rating.

options.a: NULL should be treated as zero.
options.b: Missing is a separate state from low performance.

multi-choice-1.title: Good CASE Practices
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which practices improve a row-level CASE label? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: CASE stops after the first true condition.

help.concept: Specific rules and missing-data rules should not be hidden by a broad branch.
help.hint_1: Readable labels are part of the report contract.
help.hint_2: Avoid a first branch that captures unintended rows.

options.a: Order overlapping thresholds from most specific to broadest.
options.b: Use an explicit NULL branch when missing has distinct meaning.
options.c: Choose labels that tell the reader what the category means.
options.d: Place a broad condition first so every row matches immediately.

multi-choice-2.title: Rows Labeled High Value
multi-choice-2.prompt: With `WHEN gross_sales >= 130 THEN High`, which gross values receive `High`? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-2.hint: The comparison includes the threshold itself.

help.concept: Greater-than-or-equal matches 130 and any larger value.
help.hint_1: Values below the threshold continue to later branches.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order Rating Conditions
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the CASE checks from first to last.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Handle the distinct missing state, then test higher thresholds before lower ones.

help.concept: CASE order determines which overlapping condition wins.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Close a CASE Expression
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Fill the keyword that completes the CASE before the alias.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: A CASE expression has a required closing keyword.

help.hint_1: The alias comes after the completed CASE.

fill-blank-choice-1.template: CASE WHEN discount_pct IS NULL THEN 'Missing' ELSE 'Recorded' [blank1] AS discount_state

try_case_for_readable_labels_sketch0.title: Label Discount Review Status
try_case_for_readable_labels_sketch0.prompt: Select `order_id`, `discount_pct`, and a `discount_review` label: `Missing discount` for NULL, `Large discount` for 15 or more, otherwise `Standard discount`. Return `order_id, discount_pct, CASE WHEN discount_pct IS NULL THEN 'Missing discount' WHEN discount_pct >= 15 THEN 'Large discount' ELSE 'Standard discount' END AS discount_review` from `sales_reporting` in that column order.
try_case_for_readable_labels_sketch0.hint: Put the NULL condition before the numeric threshold.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Return `order_id, discount_pct, CASE WHEN discount_pct IS NULL THEN 'Missing discount' WHEN discount_pct >= 15 THEN 'Large discount' ELSE 'Standard discount' END AS discount_review` from `sales_reporting` in that column order.

try_case_for_readable_labels_sketch0.solutionCode:
SELECT
    order_id,
    discount_pct,
    CASE
        WHEN discount_pct IS NULL THEN 'Missing discount'
        WHEN discount_pct >= 15 THEN 'Large discount'
        ELSE 'Standard discount'
    END AS discount_review
FROM sales_reporting;

try_case_for_readable_labels_sketch1.title: Classify Order Values
try_case_for_readable_labels_sketch1.prompt: Select `order_id`, gross sales, and `value_band`: `High value` for gross sales of 130 or more, `Mid value` for 80 or more, otherwise `Low value`. Return `order_id, quantity * unit_price AS gross_sales, CASE WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 130 THEN 'High value' WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 80 THEN 'Mid value' ELSE 'Low value' END AS value_band` from `sales_reporting` in that column order.
try_case_for_readable_labels_sketch1.hint: Check the highest threshold before the lower threshold.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Return `order_id, quantity * unit_price AS gross_sales, CASE WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 130 THEN 'High value' WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 80 THEN 'Mid value' ELSE 'Low value' END AS value_band` from `sales_reporting` in that column order.

try_case_for_readable_labels_sketch1.solutionCode:
SELECT
    order_id,
    quantity * unit_price AS gross_sales,
    CASE
        WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 130 THEN 'High value'
        WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 80 THEN 'Mid value'
        ELSE 'Low value'
    END AS value_band
FROM sales_reporting;

sketch0.title: Test Missing Values First
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
The `CASE` statement in SQL is a powerful tool for creating readable labels based on conditions. It allows you to evaluate a list of conditions and return a specific value when the first condition is met. If no conditions are true, it can return a default value. This is particularly useful for categorizing data in reports.

`CASE` turns conditions into readable report labels. SQL evaluates the branches from top to bottom and returns the result from the first condition that matches.

Missing values should usually be tested before numeric thresholds:

```sql
SELECT
    order_id,
    customer_rating,
    CASE
        WHEN customer_rating IS NULL THEN 'Unrated'
        WHEN customer_rating >= 4.5 THEN 'Excellent'
        WHEN customer_rating >= 4.0 THEN 'Good'
        ELSE 'Needs follow-up'
    END AS rating_label
FROM sales_reporting;
```

#### Line-by-line walkthrough

- `SELECT` — starts the result list. The indented lines define the columns and calculations returned by the query.
- `order_id,` — returns the stored `order_id` value.
- `customer_rating,` — returns the stored `customer_rating` value.
- `CASE` — starts conditional logic. SQL checks the following `WHEN` branches from top to bottom.
- `WHEN customer_rating IS NULL THEN 'Unrated'` — tests `customer_rating IS NULL` and returns `'Unrated'` when true.
- `WHEN customer_rating >= 4.5 THEN 'Excellent'` — tests `customer_rating >= 4.5` and returns `'Excellent'` when true.
- `WHEN customer_rating >= 4.0 THEN 'Good'` — tests `customer_rating >= 4.0` and returns `'Good'` when true.
- `ELSE 'Needs follow-up'` — returns `'Needs follow-up'` when no earlier condition matches.
- `END AS rating_label` — closes the `CASE` expression. The result is named `rating_label`.
- `FROM sales_reporting;` — reads source rows from the `sales_reporting` table.

A missing rating is not the same as a poor rating, so it receives its own label. The result is easier for a manager to interpret than a column of raw numbers and NULL values.

sketch1.title: Order Thresholds from Most Specific to Broadest
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
When several conditions can match the same value, place the most specific or highest threshold first.

```sql
SELECT
    order_id,
    quantity * unit_price AS gross_sales,
    CASE
        WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 150 THEN 'Large'
        WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 90 THEN 'Standard'
        ELSE 'Small'
    END AS order_size
FROM sales_reporting;
```

#### Line-by-line walkthrough

- `SELECT` — starts the result list. The indented lines define the columns and calculations returned by the query.
- `order_id,` — returns the stored `order_id` value.
- `quantity * unit_price AS gross_sales,` — multiplies row values to create a calculated measure and names the output `gross_sales`.
- `CASE` — starts conditional logic. SQL checks the following `WHEN` branches from top to bottom.
- `WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 150 THEN 'Large'` — tests `quantity * unit_price >= 150` and returns `'Large'` when true.
- `WHEN quantity * unit_price >= 90 THEN 'Standard'` — tests `quantity * unit_price >= 90` and returns `'Standard'` when true.
- `ELSE 'Small'` — returns `'Small'` when no earlier condition matches.
- `END AS order_size` — closes the `CASE` expression. The result is named `order_size`.
- `FROM sales_reporting;` — reads source rows from the `sales_reporting` table.

An order worth 170 also satisfies `>= 90`. Because the `>= 150` branch appears first, it is correctly labeled `Large`.

Read a CASE expression as a sequence of decisions. Once one branch matches, SQL stops checking the branches below it.
